summary: Gruber wraps up WWDC 2010, noting it was the most tightly focused conference ever — entirely iOS, with no Mac or IT tracks. He highlights the shift toward full-time iOS development, praises the rapid release of free session videos, and digs into the technical promise of Grand Central Dispatch and blocks. His sharpest critique targets Steve Jobs's straw-man defense of App Store rejections, arguing that the real problem — unwritten rules — went unaddressed. He also notes oddities like the bewildering FarmVille demo and Apple's decision to sell iMovie for iPhone at $5.
key_points: WWDC 2010 was the first conference with zero Mac content, reflecting Apple's singular focus on iOS
Session videos were released free to all developers just six days after the conference, fundamentally changing WWDC's dynamics
Grand Central Dispatch and blocks are becoming pervasive in Apple's APIs, valued for cleaner code design even on single-core devices
Jobs's defense of App Store rejections was a straw man — the real controversy is unwritten rules that developers discover only after building apps
The developer community is rapidly shifting from side-project iOS work to full-time iOS development, including enterprise apps
money_quotes: The tight focus of the conference was, I think, a reflection of the current focus of Apple itself. We may never see such a single-minded WWDC again.
The simple math is that there's only room at Moscone West for 5,200 attendees, but there are way more than 5,200 developers whom Apple wants to have access to these sessions.
It's a straw-man argument to hold them as a refutation of App Store criticism: no one is criticizing the App Store for rejections because of these things.
Obviously Apple can't write guidelines that cover scenarios it hasn't foreseen; but once something new comes up, their policies to handle it should be documented publicly.
The App Store review situation has improved significantly in the last year. It's getting better, not worse. But Jobs's defense of it had nothing at all to do with the aspects that remain problematic.
Eventually we will get multicore CPUs in iOS devices, and when that happens, apps written for iOS 4 will already take advantage of them.
I wonder how many WWDC attendees underestimate the quality of the consulting available from the labs.
key_insight: WWDC 2010 revealed Apple's total commitment to iOS, but Jobs's keynote defense of the App Store dodged the real issue: developers can't follow rules that aren't written down.
